epic.priority: bdo's arc, authored 2026-06-12 (chat: 'Arc — Causality as an AI-native compositional surface') and recorded here so sessions inherit it from the record, not from chat (his standing rule). It is a COMPOSING arc: it does not re-build epic.the-field or epic.experience-layer — it names the surface where both converge and adds only what neither covers (persistence/schema, the API-first interaction layer, the four-register UX discipline, the NL graph-authoring skill, and the first vertical slice). Where a piece below overlaps an existing epic's piece, its glue cites that piece and refuses to double-build (§10).
epic.arc: Causality moves from demonstration space into ontum as an AI-native, API-first compositional surface. It is not merely a visualization and not merely a control panel: it is a communication surface over a typed system — sometimes a witness mirror, sometimes a governed request surface, sometimes a composition layer for arranging atoms, nodes, virtual nodes, pulses, saved patterns, and live operational views. The prototype (the sealed causality-envoy package) proved the grammar — typed nodes, typed routes, pulses, real local inference, receipts, latency expectations — but is still demonstration-shaped: no durable persistence, no saved patterns, no virtual request nodes, no atom search, no API-first paths, no headless test seams, no honest long-running-node visibility, and no visual line between witnessed fact, proposed request, simulated possibility, and committed change. This arc lifts it into an operational surface for ontum and for other systems on the same harness pattern.
epic.context: Born from the 2026-06-12 working session that turned a portfolio toy (foundry piece 04, Causality) into a typed pulse machine, and from the sealed disclosure package exports/causality-envoy. The product principle, bdo's words: 'show less, honestly; request explicitly; compose through pens.' The log remains the territory; Causality becomes a selective, replayable, typed fold over it, with a second register for governed requests. FOUR VISIBLE REGISTERS that must never be confusable: (1) RECORD — what actually happened (atom created, verdict landed, pen wrote, gate refused) — this is ink, and it is epic.the-field's projection (atom.field-portal.v0, atom.living-flow.v0) rendered in Causality's language; (2) RUNTIME — what is happening now (node busy, pulse parked, inference running, backlog pressure, owner queue waiting) — epic.the-field's signal layer (atom.signal-layer.v0); (3) REQUEST — what a user or model asks the system to DO (create atom, search atoms, spawn review node, save pattern, launch local inference) — NOT direct mutation but a typed VIRTUAL REQUEST NODE, which is epic.experience-layer's machinery (atom.experience.v0, atom.experience-author.v0) given a first-class on-canvas form; (4) SIMULATION — what MIGHT happen (predicted pulse, proposed route, 'what if this gate rejects?', 'what if max-in-flight were 2 not 8?') — pencil, not ink. CORE OBJECT: the virtual request node — a visible node representing an intention before it becomes a durable record, carrying request kind, required fields, typed ports, permission/trust-rung requirements, a target pen, dry-run/preview behaviour, submit behaviour, and a visual lifecycle (draft, valid, invalid, submitted, accepted, refused, landed). It is the control surface made honest: it does not bypass the system; it routes every durable change through an admitted pen. API-FIRST RULE: every meaningful canvas action has an API equivalent — the browser is not the authority, the canvas is one client; read / request / projection / test API families are first-class so Chrome DevTools, Playwright, CLI, and natural language all drive the same operations. Natural language is never the source of truth: it produces typed drafts the system validates and only admitted request paths can submit.
epic.value: Causality becomes the surface where the system becomes communicable — not the log, not a picture of the log, not a second authority. Witness, request, and simulation share one canvas without sharing one truth register. A managing AI session and bdo can search real atoms, watch a real log span, compose work as typed request nodes that land only through pens, simulate a change before proposing it, and tell fact from request from possibility at a glance — all reproducible by API and provable headlessly. The system gains a place to be seen and steered without ever gaining a second way to be silently changed.
epic.horizon: Causality is no longer a demo. Its definition of done (bdo's, verbatim in intent): it can search real atoms; project a real log span; save and reload a graph view; create virtual request nodes; submit a request through a real pen; distinguish fact / request / simulation visually; handle a long-running node without lying; be driven by API, browser, and natural language; be tested headlessly; and refuse unsafe or invalid composition. The first increment proves the whole thesis on one narrow vertical slice without requiring the whole system.

pieces.status: wave 1 — the first vertical slice; bdo's chosen proof ('do not start with full production authoring')
pieces.glue: The Atom Search Request Node. A user creates or describes a virtual request node — e.g. 'search atoms related to backlog pressure and show their lifecycle' — and the system: creates a request node, validates its required fields, searches the real atom/log substrate (READ-ONLY — search witnesses, it never mutates), returns results as typed data, renders a small graph projection of their lifecycle, marks every element as record / runtime / request / simulation, saves the graph as a reusable view, and proves every step through an API call and a Playwright assertion. Proves the four registers, the virtual-request-node object, the read-only fold, persistence of a view, and the API/headless seam — all at once, on a slice that mutates nothing. Its done-line is written before the build (§9.4).

From flat in-memory demo records to durable typed graph records: a graph schema, node tagged-union schema (the prototype's flat bag of all-type fields is named in the envoy seams as not surviving a database — this is the fix), route schema, pulse schema, request-node schema, saved-pattern schema, versioning + migration, import/export, fixtures + replay tests. Acceptance: a saved pattern can be created, versioned, instantiated into a graph, replayed from storage, and compared against an expected projection.

Causality renders ontum from the log: log-to-graph, atom-lifecycle, epic-topology, active-run, and pressure/heat/queue projections, with an explicit omitted-detail policy and collapse/expand subgraphs. DO NOT double-build (§10): this IS epic.the-field's work — atom.field-topology.v0 (the deterministic fold, value-confirmed 2026-06-12, parked at owner-stamp pending bdo's confirm of epic.the-field) generalised, and atom.field-portal.v0 / atom.living-flow.v0 rendered in Causality's grammar. This piece is the rendering contract over the-field's fold, not a second fold. Acceptance: given a fixed log fixture, a deterministic graph projection with stable ids and replayable collapsed state.

pieces.status: wave 3 — control by request, not mutation (workstream C)
pieces.glue: The virtual request node lifecycle as a first-class engine object: validation states, target-pen mapping, required-field inspection, submit-to-pen behaviour, refusal and receipt rendering, permission / trust-rung checks. DO NOT double-build (§10): the submission machinery IS epic.experience-layer (atom.experience.v0 = the typed request/experience record; atom.experience-author.v0 = self-extension proposes, owner admits, through a pen that cannot emit a non-story; atom.trust-ladder.v0 = the rung checks). This piece gives that machinery an on-canvas form. Acceptance: creating a node creates a request object, not a mutation; submitting either lands through the correct pen with a receipt or refuses with a legible reason. The core invariant lives or dies here: request, compose, preview, witness — but durable change lands only through admitted pens, logged records, owner-authorised seams (D-4).

First-class parked/resumed pulses, node busy state, cancellation, timeout, retry, backpressure, queueing, max-in-flight, latency expectations, progress events. The envoy seams name the prototype's concurrency model as informal (a pulse is consumed and a new one emitted; a second pulse mid-think is dropped) — this replaces the visual metaphor with a runtime. Acceptance: a graph with several slow local-inference nodes runs with no dropped pulses while the UI shows what is parked, busy, cancellable, and waiting on owner/system capacity.

A local HTTP API with typed request/response contracts and a published schema (OpenAPI or equivalent); read / request / projection / test families; Playwright fixtures; a browser-debug surface; CLI wrappers; worked examples for both mechanical and natural-language operation. Every major gesture reproducible by an API call and verified by a test. The browser stops being the authority; the canvas becomes one client among CLI, DevTools, Playwright, and NL.

An AI skill that reads the repo, understands available atoms/pens/patterns, and proposes VALID graph/request specs from natural language: a graph-spec output schema, atom-search behaviour, pattern recommendation, refusal rules, a validation loop, and model-review + red-team packets. Natural language produces typed drafts, never truth. Acceptance: given a task in words, the skill searches current atoms, proposes a schema-valid graph/request spec, and explains what will be WITNESSED versus what will be REQUESTED.

Make witness / control / simulation visually impossible to confuse: a visual grammar for record/runtime/request/simulation, node badges, route states, pulse states, request styling (pending/submitted/refused), committed-receipt styling, collapse/expand, and per-element configuration panels (click a node → config for its type; click a route → its config — the immediate UX gap bdo named, drawing on the foundry's 30+ vetted interaction exemplars and the five built design skills rather than inventing it). Acceptance: a user can tell, without reading logs, whether a thing is fact, live process, request, or simulation.
